part_subtitle: Field Decision-Making Matrix for Tuta absoluta Management

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction
Decision-making is the central point in integrated management of Tuta absoluta, because the efficacy of any control measure depends largely on selecting:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Decision-making is the central point in integrated management of Tuta absoluta, because the efficacy of any control measure depends largely on selecting:

items.text: A farm may have:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A farm may have:

items.text: Yet the programme may still fail when decisions are made:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Yet the programme may still fail when decisions are made:

items: On the basis of one indicator.
Without considering crop stage and harvest.
Without reviewing the history of insecticide groups.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Without reviewing the history of insecticide groups.

items.text: The decision matrix converts field data into a logical sequence that determines:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision matrix converts field data into a logical sequence that determines:

items: 5. Is the infestation internal or caused by immigration?
6. What is the least disruptive action capable of preventing loss?
7. When should the outcome be assessed?
8. What is the next action if the expected reduction is not achieved?

[[prepared]]6. What is the least disruptive action capable of preventing loss?
[[prepared]]7. When should the outcome be assessed?

items.text: The matrix is not a spray schedule or fixed prescription. It is a decision-making system that changes according to field conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The matrix is not a spray schedule or fixed prescription. It is a decision-making system that changes according to field conditions.

items.text: Difference Between the Matrix and a Conventional Schedule
Conventional Spray Schedule
This often depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This often depends on:

items: Applying treatment regardless of pest status.

This depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This depends on:

items.text: This means that two fields transplanted on the same day may require different decisions when they differ in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This means that two fields transplanted on the same day may require different decisions when they differ in:

items.text: Objectives of the Decision Matrix
The matrix aims to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The matrix aims to:

items: Detect the need for intervention early.
Direct control towards eggs and young larvae.
Distinguish local hotspots from external immigration waves.
Reduce errors arising from trap interpretation.
Link control decisions to harvest timing.
Standardize the reasoning process among technical-team members.
Document the reason for every decision.
Facilitate review of success or failure.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Direct control towards eggs and young larvae.
[[prepared]]Distinguish local hotspots from external immigration waves.
[[prepared]]Standardize the reasoning process among technical-team members.

items.text: Basic Principles of Decision-Making
The matrix is based on the following principles:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The matrix is based on the following principles:

items: 1. No spray decision should be made from traps alone.
2. Decisions should not be based on old inactive mines.
3. Presence of eggs does not always require a chemical insecticide.
4. Larval instar is more important than count alone when selecting an intervention.
5. Fruit infestation is economically more serious than a similar number of leaf mines.
6. Parasitism percentage should not be interpreted separately from total egg density.
7. Natural enemies are part of the intervention decision, not an additional note.
8. Crop stage determines the acceptable level of damage.
10. High infestation along field edges may indicate external pressure.
11. Treatment failure does not automatically mean resistance.
12. Rotation is based on IRAC group number, not trade name.
13. Every intervention must be followed by a defined evaluation.
14. Non-intervention is a valid technical decision when there is no economic or biological justification.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. No spray decision should be made from traps alone.
[[prepared]]2. Decisions should not be based on old inactive mines.
[[prepared]]3. Presence of eggs does not always require a chemical insecticide.
[[prepared]]4. Larval instar is more important than count alone when selecting an intervention.
[[prepared]]5. Fruit infestation is economically more serious than a similar number of leaf mines.
[[prepared]]6. Parasitism percentage should not be interpreted separately from total egg density.
[[prepared]]7. Natural enemies are part of the intervention decision, not an additional note.
[[prepared]]8. Crop stage determines the acceptable level of damage.
[[prepared]]10. High infestation along field edges may indicate external pressure.
[[prepared]]11. Treatment failure does not automatically mean resistance.
[[prepared]]12. Rotation is based on IRAC group number, not trade name.
[[prepared]]13. Every intervention must be followed by a defined evaluation.
[[prepared]]14. Non-intervention is a valid technical decision when there is no economic or biological justification.

items.text: Required Data Sources
The matrix is not built from one reading, but from six principal data groups.

items.text: Decision Unit
The entire field should not be treated as one decision unit when clear differences exist.

items.text: Verifying Data Validity
Before interpreting results, answer the following questions:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before interpreting results, answer the following questions:

items: Is the lure within its effective life?
Was a sufficient number of plants inspected?
Did the sample include both edges and interior?
Do mines classified as active contain evidence of activity?
Were Tuta eggs distinguished from eggs of other insects?
Was larval instar identified with reasonable accuracy?
Do the data belong to the same sector?
Was a recent treatment applied that may alter the result?
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Is the lure within its effective life?
[[prepared]]Was a sufficient number of plants inspected?
[[prepared]]Did the sample include both edges and interior?
[[prepared]]Do mines classified as active contain evidence of activity?
[[prepared]]Were Tuta eggs distinguished from eggs of other insects?
[[prepared]]Was larval instar identified with reasonable accuracy?
[[prepared]]Do the data belong to the same sector?
[[prepared]]Was a recent treatment applied that may alter the result?

items.text: When data quality is poor, the correct decision is often:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When data quality is poor, the correct decision is often:

items.text: Repeat the inspection and improve measurement quality
Rather than proceed directly to spraying.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rather than proceed directly to spraying.

items.text: Trend Is More Important Than a Single Reading
The following should be analysed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be analysed:

items.text: A high reading may be less serious if it is clearly declining, whereas a moderate reading may be more important if it rises week after week and coincides with fresh egg appearance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A high reading may be less serious if it is clearly declining, whereas a moderate reading may be more important if it rises week after week and coincides with fresh egg appearance.

items.text: Trends may be classified as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trends may be classified as:

items: High after removal of a neighbouring field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]High after removal of a neighbouring field.

items.text: A number should not be interpreted without its trend and context.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A number should not be interpreted without its trend and context.

items.text: General Decision Levels
Field status is divided into five levels.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field status is divided into five levels.

rows: No active infestation or only limited stable evidence
Early-warning indicators without confirmed economic damage
Active infestation still containable before reaching fruits
Spread threatening growing points, clusters, or fruits
Advanced infestation, repeated failure, or field becoming a source
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]No [[term:infestation-status]]active infestation[[/term]] or only limited stable evidence
[[prepared]]Active infestation still containable before reaching fruits
[[prepared]]Spread threatening growing points, clusters, or fruits
[[prepared]]Advanced infestation, repeated failure, or field becoming a source

items.text: These levels are not substitutes for an economic threshold, but a framework for standardizing response.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These levels are not substitutes for an [[term:economic-threshold]]economic threshold[[/term]], but a framework for standardizing response.

items.text: Green Level
Definition of the Green Level
A field is classified as green when most of the following indicators occur together:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A field is classified as green when most of the following indicators occur together:

items: Trap catches are low or stable.
No consecutive increase in catch is present.
Eggs are absent or extremely scattered.
Live larvae are not widely distributed.
No nearby high-risk source field exists.
No recent immigration wave has occurred.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]No consecutive increase in catch is present.

items.text: Green does not mean the pest is completely absent from the region. It means there is no justification for corrective intervention.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Green does not mean the pest is completely absent from the region. It means there is no justification for corrective intervention.

items.text: Green-Level Actions
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: 4. Remove isolated suspect plants or fruits where present.
6. Do not apply unjustified preventive sprays.
9. Prepare intervention alternatives without using them.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]4. Remove isolated suspect plants or fruits where present.
[[prepared]]6. Do not apply unjustified preventive sprays.
[[prepared]]9. Prepare intervention alternatives without using them.

items.text: The principal decision is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The principal decision is:

items.text: Active monitoring without chemical intervention.
Yellow Level
Definition of the Yellow Level
The yellow level represents early warning and may apply when one or more of the following indicators are present:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The yellow level represents early warning and may apply when one or more of the following indicators are present:

items: Catch rises along one field edge.
A neighbouring crop begins to be removed.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A neighbouring crop begins to be removed.

items.text: Yellow does not mean that spraying is required. It means establishment of a new generation must be prevented.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Yellow does not mean that spraying is required. It means establishment of a new generation must be prevented.

items.text: Yellow-Level Actions
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: 2. Increase the number of plants inspected.
3. Inspect the field edge and interior separately.
9. Prepare egg parasitoids or the appropriate microbial agent.
10. Review the IRAC record and preharvest intervals.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]2. Increase the number of plants inspected.
[[prepared]]3. Inspect the field edge and interior separately.
[[prepared]]9. Prepare egg parasitoids or the appropriate microbial agent.
[[prepared]]10. Review the IRAC record and preharvest intervals.

items.text: The usual decision is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The usual decision is:

items.text: Intensify monitoring and prepare for early intervention without automatic whole-field treatment.
When Does Yellow Become Orange?
The level changes when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The level changes when:

items: Egg laying continues in two consecutive inspections.
The number of plants carrying eggs increases.
Parasitism falls below the level capable of containing the eggs.
Infestation appears near growing points or clusters.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Egg laying continues in two consecutive inspections.
[[prepared]]The number of plants carrying eggs increases.
[[prepared]]Parasitism falls below the level capable of containing the eggs.
[[prepared]]Infestation appears near growing points or clusters.

items.text: The decision should not wait until large larvae appear.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should not wait until large larvae appear.

items.text: Orange Level
Definition of the Orange Level
Orange represents an active infestation that remains containable before major loss occurs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Orange represents an active infestation that remains containable before major loss occurs.

items.text: Its indicators include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its indicators include:

items: No fruit infestation or only very early cases.
Natural enemies are present but insufficient alone.
Time remains to intervene before larvae enter deeper tissues.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]No fruit infestation or only very early cases.
[[prepared]]Natural enemies are present but insufficient alone.
[[prepared]]Time remains to intervene before larvae enter deeper tissues.

items.text: This level is the most important opportunity for preventing an outbreak.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This level is the most important opportunity for preventing an outbreak.

items.text: Orange-Level Actions
Actions should be arranged from least disruptive to more intensive:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Actions should be arranged from least disruptive to more intensive:

items: 1. Release or augment egg parasitoids when egg stage and residues are suitable.
2. Use Bt or a registered microbial agent targeting young larvae.
4. Remove limited hotspots or early infested fruits.
6. Treat affected edges or sectors where separation is possible.
7. Use a registered selective insecticide if infestation trend exceeds the capacity of biological methods.
9. Define the reassessment date before most larvae reach advanced instars.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Release or augment egg parasitoids when egg stage and residues are suitable.
[[prepared]]2. Use Bt or a registered microbial agent targeting young larvae.
[[prepared]]4. Remove limited hotspots or early infested fruits.
[[prepared]]6. Treat affected edges or sectors where separation is possible.
[[prepared]]7. Use a registered selective insecticide if infestation trend exceeds the capacity of biological methods.
[[prepared]]9. Define the reassessment date before most larvae reach advanced instars.

items.text: An orange-level response should not become a broad chemical mixture merely to achieve rapid kill.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An orange-level response should not become a broad chemical mixture merely to achieve rapid kill.

items.text: Decision When Egg Density and Parasitism Are Both High
Parasitism percentage should not be interpreted separately from total egg number.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Parasitism percentage should not be interpreted separately from total egg number.

items.text: Conceptual example:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conceptual example:

items: A very large egg population with high parasitism may still leave a substantial number of viable eggs.
A limited egg population with moderate parasitism may produce fewer larvae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A very large egg population with high parasitism may still leave a substantial number of viable eggs.
[[prepared]]A limited egg population with moderate parasitism may produce fewer larvae.

items.text: The decision should consider:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should consider:

items.text: The decision may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision may be:

items: Combine this with a selective agent against hatching larvae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Combine this with a selective agent against hatching larvae.

items.text: A pesticide highly toxic to egg parasitoids should not be used merely because total egg density is high.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A pesticide highly toxic to egg parasitoids should not be used merely because total egg density is high.

items.text: Decision When Egg Density Is High and Parasitism Is Low
This condition indicates the possibility of a large larval wave.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This condition indicates the possibility of a large larval wave.

items.text: It requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It requires:

items: Confirming that eggs are alive and fresh.
Timing Bt or another larval agent before or near hatching.
Checking for pesticide residues toxic to parasitoids.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Confirming that eggs are alive and fresh.
[[prepared]]Timing Bt or another larval agent before or near hatching.
[[prepared]]Checking for pesticide residues toxic to parasitoids.

items.text: The priority is to prevent hatching or kill larvae immediately after feeding begins.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The priority is to prevent hatching or kill larvae immediately after feeding begins.

items.text: Decision When L1 and L2 Dominate
This is the best opportunity for larval control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the best opportunity for larval control.

items.text: Potential measures include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Potential measures include:

items: A registered and suitable Bt product.
A selective insecticide effective against early instars.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A selective insecticide effective against early instars.

items.text: Good coverage is required at:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Good coverage is required at:

items.text: Intervention should not be delayed until completion of a full weekly evaluation when temperature permits rapid development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intervention should not be delayed until completion of a full weekly evaluation when temperature permits rapid development.

items.text: Red Level
Definition of the Red Level
A field is classified as red when there is a direct threat to the crop, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A field is classified as red when there is a direct threat to the crop, such as:

items: Increasing mines despite a previous intervention.
Infestation approaching an economically damaging level.

items.text: The objective is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The objective is:

items.text: To stop the increase and protect sound fruits, not merely reduce moth numbers.
Red-Level Actions
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: 1. Identify the most heavily infested sectors.
3. Use a registered corrective measure effective against the dominant stage.
4. Select an IRAC group different from the previous window.
5. Protect natural enemies as far as possible.
6. Coordinate management of the external source field.
9. Collect samples where resistance is suspected.
11. Prevent rejected fruits from being left between rows.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Identify the most heavily infested sectors.
[[prepared]]3. Use a registered corrective measure effective against the dominant stage.
[[prepared]]4. Select an IRAC group different from the previous window.
[[prepared]]5. Protect natural enemies as far as possible.
[[prepared]]6. Coordinate management of the external source field.
[[prepared]]9. Collect samples where resistance is suspected.
[[prepared]]11. Prevent rejected fruits from being left between rows.

items.text: The red level does not justify exceeding the label rate or randomly mixing several products.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The red level does not justify exceeding the label rate or randomly mixing several products.

items.text: Decision When L3 and L4 Dominate
When advanced instars dominate, recognize that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When advanced instars dominate, recognize that:

items: Some larvae are inside large mines.
Some may be moving into fruits.
Bt may be less effective than against young larvae.
Part of the damage may already have occurred.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Bt may be less effective than against young larvae.
[[prepared]]Part of the damage may already have occurred.

items.text: The response should include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The response should include:

items: Using a registered material proven active against the stage present.
Avoiding repetition of the group that failed.
Analysing why larvae reached these instars without detection.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Using a registered material proven active against the stage present.
[[prepared]]Avoiding repetition of the group that failed.
[[prepared]]Analysing why larvae reached these instars without detection.

items.text: Mortality of some large larvae after treatment should not be interpreted as complete recovery of the damaged crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mortality of some large larvae after treatment should not be interpreted as complete recovery of the damaged crop.

items.text: Decision at the Beginning of Fruit Infestation
The first confirmed fruit infestation marks a major increase in risk.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The first confirmed fruit infestation marks a major increase in risk.

items.text: The following should be done:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be done:

items: 1. Determine the percentage of infested fruits.
2. Confirm whether live larvae are present.
4. Identify larval instars present on leaves.
5. Collect infested fruits and do not leave them in the field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Determine the percentage of infested fruits.
[[prepared]]2. Confirm whether live larvae are present.
[[prepared]]4. Identify larval instars present on leaves.
[[prepared]]5. Collect infested fruits and do not leave them in the field.

items.text: The programme should be adjusted before fruit infestation becomes widespread.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The programme should be adjusted before fruit infestation becomes widespread.

items.text: Critical Level
Definition of the Critical Level
This represents a condition beyond routine intervention and may include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This represents a condition beyond routine intervention and may include:

items: High densities of several insect stages.
Failure of one or more treatments.
A heavily infested old field producing large numbers of emigrating moths.
Inability to comply with the preharvest interval of some options.
Declining economic viability of continuing the crop.
Transformation of the field into a regional source.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A heavily infested old field producing large numbers of emigrating moths.
[[prepared]]Inability to comply with the preharvest interval of some options.
[[prepared]]Declining economic viability of continuing the crop.
[[prepared]]Transformation of the field into a regional source.

items.text: This level requires an administrative and economic decision, not merely selection of an insecticide.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This level requires an administrative and economic decision, not merely selection of an insecticide.

items.text: Critical-Level Actions
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: 3. Determine fruit infestation and expected loss.
6. Stop use of the group suspected of failure.
7. Use a registered alternative with a different mode of action when economically justified.
14. Document the causes leading to the critical condition.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]3. Determine fruit infestation and expected loss.
[[prepared]]6. Stop use of the group suspected of failure.
[[prepared]]7. Use a registered alternative with a different [[term:mode-of-action]]mode of action[[/term]] when economically justified.
[[prepared]]14. Document the causes leading to the critical condition.

items.text: Terminating a severely infested sector may be more economical than continuing repeated sprays that cannot protect an economic crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Terminating a severely infested sector may be more economical than continuing repeated sprays that cannot protect an economic crop.

rows: Continue monitoring and protect beneficial organisms
Intensify inspection and review the source
Egg parasitoids and a compatible early-stage agent
Early selective intervention and rapid evaluation
Treat the hotspot rather than the entire field
Containment, resistance investigation, and economic evaluation
Do not cancel action because trap catch is low
Do not spray from trap data alone; identify the immigration source

items.text: Decision-Making in the Nursery
The operational threshold in the nursery is extremely low because infested transplants can distribute the pest over large areas.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The operational threshold in the nursery is extremely low because infested transplants can distribute the pest over large areas.

items.text: When No Infestation Is Present
When One Suspect Plant Is Present
When Infestation Is Distributed

items: Reject the batch if freedom from live stages cannot be guaranteed.

Priority is given to identifying the infestation source.

This suggests association with the nursery or batch.

This suggests immigration pressure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This suggests immigration pressure.

items.text: Randomly Distributed Infestation Within the Field
This requires review of transplant history and moth arrival.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This requires review of transplant history and moth arrival.

items.text: Intervention should be early and localized where possible, while preventing spread into new growing points.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intervention should be early and localized where possible, while preventing spread into new growing points.

items.text: Decision-Making During Flowering and Fruit Set
Risk should be increased when the following occur:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Risk should be increased when the following occur:

items.text: The decision should be less tolerant of delay because damage can move rapidly from leaf to fruit.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should be less tolerant of delay because damage can move rapidly from leaf to fruit.

items.text: Decision-Making During the Fruit Stage
The following indicators should be included:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following indicators should be included:

items.text: The decision should be based on the value of fruits already present, not only expectations of vegetative recovery.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should be based on the value of fruits already present, not only expectations of vegetative recovery.

items.text: Decision-Making Near Harvest
Before any intervention, review:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before any intervention, review:

items: 8. Time required for the measure to act.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]8. Time required for the measure to act.

items.text: A slow-acting method should not be used when harvest will begin before benefit appears, unless the objective is to prevent the field becoming a postharvest source.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A slow-acting method should not be used when harvest will begin before benefit appears, unless the objective is to prevent the field becoming a postharvest source.

items.text: Effect of Neighbouring Fields
External risk should be rated high when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]External risk should be rated high when:

items: A heavily infested old field is present.
Spraying has stopped in that field.
Trap catches increase on the facing edge.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A heavily infested old field is present.
[[prepared]]Trap catches increase on the facing edge.

items.text: This does not automatically require preventive spraying, but it does require:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not automatically require preventive spraying, but it does require:

items.text: Immigration Wave Versus Internal Reproduction
Evidence of External Immigration

items: Increase associated with removal of a neighbouring field.
Lower infestation in the field interior.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Increase associated with removal of a neighbouring field.

items.text: Evidence of Internal Reproduction

items: Presence of pupae or a succeeding generation.
Continued oviposition without a clear external source.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Presence of pupae or a succeeding generation.
[[prepared]]Continued oviposition without a clear external source.

items.text: Both processes may occur together, requiring management of the external source and the internal generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Both processes may occur together, requiring management of the external source and the internal generation.

items.text: Natural-Enemy and Intervention Matrix

rows: Eggs high but few remain unparasitized
Stop the disruptive material and rebuild the programme
Measure before deciding when time permits
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Stop the disruptive material and rebuild the programme

items.text: The presence of one natural enemy on several plants does not mean natural regulation is sufficient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The presence of one natural enemy on several plants does not mean natural regulation is sufficient.

items.text: Selecting the Type of Intervention
Monitoring Only
Selected when no active infestation exists or the trend is stable and low.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selected when no active infestation exists or the trend is stable and low.

items.text: Intensified Prevention
Includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Includes:

items.text: Biological Intervention
Selected when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selected when:

items: A suitable pest stage is present.
Pest density is low or moderate.
Sufficient time exists for the effect to appear.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Sufficient time exists for the effect to appear.

items.text: Microbial Intervention
Selected when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selected when:

items: Eggs are near hatching or young larvae are present.
A viable registered product is available.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Eggs are near hatching or young larvae are present.

items.text: Localized Intervention
Selected when infestation is spatially limited.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selected when infestation is spatially limited.

items.text: Selective Chemical Intervention
Selected when damage is likely to exceed the capacity of natural regulation or threatens fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selected when damage is likely to exceed the capacity of natural regulation or threatens fruits.

items.text: Containment or Sector Termination
Selected when economic viability declines or the sector becomes a source of risk.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selected when economic viability declines or the sector becomes a source of risk.

items.text: Prioritizing Among Multiple Options
Selection should follow these criteria:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selection should follow these criteria:

items: 1. Capacity to target the dominant stage.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Capacity to target the dominant stage.

items.text: The least expensive product should not be selected if it cannot reach the stage present or causes loss of natural control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The least expensive product should not be selected if it cannot reach the stage present or causes loss of natural control.

items.text: Reviewing the IRAC Record Before a Decision
Before any insecticide is used, record:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before any insecticide is used, record:

items: Group used against the previous generation.

items.text: Switching between active ingredients within the same group is not true rotation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Switching between active ingredients within the same group is not true rotation.

items.text: Declining efficacy should not be addressed by repeating the same group under a different trade name.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Declining efficacy should not be addressed by repeating the same group under a different trade name.

items.text: Reviewing the Preharvest Interval
As harvest approaches, exclude any option that does not permit compliance with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]As harvest approaches, exclude any option that does not permit compliance with:

items.text: A short preharvest interval does not mean the material is biologically optimal; it may not target the stage present or may harm natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A short preharvest interval does not mean the material is biologically optimal; it may not target the stage present or may harm natural enemies.

items.text: Defining the Reassessment Date
The reassessment date should be set before treatment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The reassessment date should be set before treatment.

items.text: It depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It depends on:

items.text: Reassessment should be more rapid when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reassessment should be more rapid when:

items.text: One interval should not be used for all control methods.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One interval should not be used for all control methods.

items.text: Indicators of Intervention Success
An intervention is successful when one or more of the following occur:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An intervention is successful when one or more of the following occur:

items: Infestation does not spread to new sectors.
No rapid unplanned repeat treatment is required.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Infestation does not spread to new sectors.
[[prepared]]No rapid unplanned repeat treatment is required.

items.text: Disappearance of moths from traps alone is not evidence of success.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Disappearance of moths from traps alone is not evidence of success.

items.text: Indicators of Intervention Failure
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: Continued appearance of L1 after the expected action time.
New mines appearing in several sectors.
Live larvae remaining in well-covered areas.
Need to repeat the same group.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Continued appearance of L1 after the expected action time.

items.text: The following should be distinguished:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be distinguished:

items.text: Failure-Investigation Tree
When a treatment fails, follow this sequence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When a treatment fails, follow this sequence.

items.text: Question One: Was the Measured Infestation New?
If mines were old, there is insufficient evidence of failure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If mines were old, there is insufficient evidence of failure.

items.text: Question Two: Was the Target Stage Appropriate?
A product intended for early instars may fail against L4 inside fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A product intended for early instars may fail against L4 inside fruits.

items.text: Question Three: Did the Material Reach the Target?
Review coverage and calibration.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Review coverage and calibration.

items.text: Question Four: Did Weather Affect the Treatment?
Review wind, rainfall, and temperature.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Review wind, rainfall, and temperature.

items.text: Question Five: Was the Registered Dose Used?
Review calculations and preparation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Review calculations and preparation.

items.text: Question Six: Is There a New Immigration Wave?
Review field edges, traps, and neighbouring fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Review field edges, traps, and neighbouring fields.

items.text: Question Seven: Was the Group Repeated?
Review the IRAC window.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Review the IRAC window.

items.text: Question Eight: Is Resistance Present?
Collect samples and conduct susceptibility testing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Collect samples and conduct susceptibility testing.

items.text: The investigation should not jump directly to the final question.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The investigation should not jump directly to the final question.

items.text: Optional Internal Scoring System
A project may create a scoring system to help rank fields, provided it is not treated as a validated economic threshold.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A project may create a scoring system to help rank fields, provided it is not treated as a validated economic threshold.

items.text: Example:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Example:

items.text: Scores should not be summed mechanically to trigger spraying. They may be used to identify:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Scores should not be summed mechanically to trigger spraying. They may be used to identify:

items.text: The system should be calibrated using Egyptian field results before adoption.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The system should be calibrated using Egyptian field results before adoption.

items.text: Why Is There No General Numerical Threshold for Egypt?
Because intervention levels change with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Because intervention levels change with:

items.text: Published studies have produced different economic-threshold values under different countries, environments, and production systems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Published studies have produced different economic-threshold values under different countries, environments, and production systems.

items.text: A figure expressed as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A figure expressed as:

items.text: Should therefore not be used as a general Egyptian threshold without local trials.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Should therefore not be used as a general Egyptian threshold without local trials.

items.text: Developing a Local Threshold
Development of an Egyptian threshold requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Development of an Egyptian threshold requires:

items: 1. Selecting a defined region and production cycle.
2. Selecting a known cultivar and production system.
11. Repeating the experiment over two or more seasons.
12. Updating the threshold as prices and resistance change.

[[prepared]]2. Selecting a known cultivar and production system.

[[prepared]]12. Updating the threshold as prices and resistance change.

The record should also include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The record should also include:

items: Previous active ingredient and IRAC group.

Before issuing a spray order, complete the following information.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before issuing a spray order, complete the following information.

items.text: Evidence of Need
Evaluation of Alternatives

items: Can a biological agent be used?
What is the status of natural enemies?
prepared_items: [[prepared]]What is the status of natural enemies?

items: Was it used against the previous generation?
What is its effect on natural enemies?
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Was it used against the previous generation?
[[prepared]]What is its effect on natural enemies?

items: When will the field be reinspected?

items.text: A spray order should not be issued without a reviewable justification.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A spray order should not be issued without a reviewable justification.

items.text: Non-Intervention Decision Card
Record that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Record that:

items: Trap catches do not show a persistent increasing trend.
No substantial live egg population is present.
Live larvae are absent or limited.
No high-risk external source is present.
Reinspection will occur on the specified date.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Trap catches do not show a persistent increasing trend.
[[prepared]]No substantial live egg population is present.
[[prepared]]Reinspection will occur on the specified date.

items.text: Documenting non-intervention demonstrates that it is based on monitoring rather than absence of follow-up.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Documenting non-intervention demonstrates that it is based on monitoring rather than absence of follow-up.

items.text: Concise Scenario Matrix

rows: L3 and L4 in growing points
Collect infested fruit and protect sound fruit
Mines remain after treatment but no larvae
Live larvae after a well-applied treatment
Comprehensive investigation and change of MoA
Removal of an adjacent old field
Collect infested fruit and review PHI
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Collect infested fruit and protect sound fruit
[[prepared]]Mines remain after treatment but no larvae

items.text: Decision-Making in Processing Tomatoes
Particular priority should be given to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Particular priority should be given to:

items: Fruit infestation by number and weight.

items.text: The decision should not be based on leaf mines alone when fruits remain sound and the canopy can still perform its function.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should not be based on leaf mines alone when fruits remain sound and the canopy can still perform its function.

items.text: Conversely, limited fruit infestation should not be underestimated when it indicates a wave likely to expand before harvest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conversely, limited fruit infestation should not be underestimated when it indicates a wave likely to expand before harvest.

items.text: Decision-Making in Fresh-Market Tomatoes
Decision sensitivity is higher because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Decision sensitivity is higher because of:

items: Low acceptance of fruits with holes.
Possibility of a larva inside a fruit that appears partly sound.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Possibility of a larva inside a fruit that appears partly sound.

items.text: The programme should:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The programme should:

items: Review the most recent treatment before every picking.
Avoid any product incompatible with the harvest programme.
Give greater weight to fruit-infestation indicators.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Review the most recent treatment before every picking.
[[prepared]]Avoid any product incompatible with the harvest programme.

items.text: Adjusting the Matrix According to Temperature
When temperature is favourable for rapid pest development:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When temperature is favourable for rapid pest development:

items: Orange may become red within a short period.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Orange may become red within a short period.

items.text: Under cooler conditions:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under cooler conditions:

items: Egg and larval stages may persist longer.
One generation may remain in the crop for a longer period.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Egg and larval stages may persist longer.
[[prepared]]One generation may remain in the crop for a longer period.

items.text: Reassessment timing should therefore be linked to thermal units and local temperature, not calendar time alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reassessment timing should therefore be linked to thermal units and local temperature, not calendar time alone.

items.text: Common Errors
Error One: Treating Trap Catch as a Spray Order
Traps are warning tools, not an independent general threshold.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps are warning tools, not an independent general threshold.

items.text: Error Two: Ignoring Plant Data When Trap Catch Is Low
Mated females or an internal generation may be present.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mated females or an internal generation may be present.

items.text: Error Three: Counting Old Mines
This leads to unnecessary repeat treatments.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This leads to unnecessary repeat treatments.

items.text: Error Four: Failing to Identify Larval Instar
This makes intervention selection imprecise.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This makes intervention selection imprecise.

items.text: Error Five: Relying on Parasitism Percentage Alone
Expected surviving eggs should also be calculated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Expected surviving eggs should also be calculated.

items.text: Error Six: Ignoring Fruit Infestation
It may be more serious than leaf damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may be more serious than leaf damage.

items.text: Error Seven: Using One Threshold for All Crop Stages
Plant tolerance changes with development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plant tolerance changes with development.

items.text: Error Eight: Treating Predator Presence as a Permanent Reason Not to Intervene
Predator density may be insufficient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Predator density may be insufficient.

items.text: Error Nine: Using the Matrix as a Rigid Schedule
It is a framework requiring data and interpretation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is a framework requiring data and interpretation.

items.text: Error Ten: Summing Indicator Scores and Automatically Triggering a Spray
The decision requires biological and economic review.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision requires biological and economic review.

items.text: Error Eleven: Ignoring Neighbouring Fields
Pressure may be external and continuous.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pressure may be external and continuous.

items.text: Error Twelve: Failing to Define a Reassessment Date
This prevents determination of success or failure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This prevents determination of success or failure.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Repeating the Same Group After Failure
This increases resistance pressure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This increases resistance pressure.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Using a Product near Harvest Without Reviewing the PHI
This may cause crop rejection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may cause crop rejection.

items.text: Error Fifteen: Failing to Document the Reason for the Decision
This makes seasonal analysis and improvement difficult.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This makes seasonal analysis and improvement difficult.

items.text: Practical Priorities in Egypt

items: 1. Develop a separate matrix for each region and production cycle.
2. Standardize inspection of eggs, mines, and fruits.
3. Separate field edges from the interior.
4. Record trap trend rather than raw catch alone.
5. Do not establish a universal spray threshold from traps.
6. Develop local thresholds for processing tomatoes.
7. Develop separate thresholds for fresh-market tomatoes.
8. Introduce fruit infestation into the decision at an early stage.
12. Include immigration source in the risk level.
13. Link the decision to the nursery record.
14. Review IRAC groups before every intervention.
15. Link the decision to harvest timing and residue requirements.
17. Set a defined assessment date for every action.
18. Train teams to distinguish old from new mines.
19. Analyse non-intervention decisions as carefully as intervention decisions.
20. Convert the matrix into a standardized paper and digital form.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Develop a separate matrix for each region and production cycle.
[[prepared]]2. Standardize inspection of eggs, mines, and fruits.
[[prepared]]3. Separate field edges from the interior.
[[prepared]]4. Record trap trend rather than raw catch alone.
[[prepared]]5. Do not establish a universal spray threshold from traps.
[[prepared]]6. Develop local thresholds for processing tomatoes.
[[prepared]]7. Develop separate thresholds for fresh-market tomatoes.
[[prepared]]8. Introduce fruit infestation into the decision at an early stage.
[[prepared]]12. Include immigration source in the risk level.
[[prepared]]13. Link the decision to the nursery record.
[[prepared]]14. Review IRAC groups before every intervention.
[[prepared]]15. Link the decision to harvest timing and residue requirements.
[[prepared]]17. Set a defined assessment date for every action.
[[prepared]]18. Train teams to distinguish old from new mines.
[[prepared]]19. Analyse non-intervention decisions as carefully as intervention decisions.
[[prepared]]20. Convert the matrix into a standardized paper and digital form.

items.text: Scientific Summary of Section Seventeen
The decision-making matrix is a system for integrating data on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision-making matrix is a system for integrating data on:

items.text: A control decision should not be made from a single indicator.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A control decision should not be made from a single indicator.

items.text: The matrix uses five levels:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The matrix uses five levels:

items: Critical for containment, investigation, and assessment of crop continuation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Critical for containment, investigation, and assessment of crop continuation.

items.text: The orange level is the most important opportunity for success because it often coincides with eggs and young larvae before they reach fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The orange level is the most important opportunity for success because it often coincides with eggs and young larvae before they reach fruits.

items.text: High trap catch does not require spraying when eggs or plant infestation are absent. Likewise, low trap catch does not mean the field is safe when larvae or infested fruits are present.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High trap catch does not require spraying when eggs or plant infestation are absent. Likewise, low trap catch does not mean the field is safe when larvae or infested fruits are present.

items.text: The following should be distinguished:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be distinguished:

items.text: Crop stage determines risk. Larvae near clusters and fruits are more serious than a similar number on leaves of a young plant capable of compensation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop stage determines risk. Larvae near clusters and fruits are more serious than a similar number on leaves of a young plant capable of compensation.

items.text: Natural enemies should be incorporated into the decision without assuming they are always capable of containing the infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Natural enemies should be incorporated into the decision without assuming they are always capable of containing the infestation.

items.text: No universal fixed numerical threshold can be used for all tomato fields in Egypt because the decision changes with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No universal fixed numerical threshold can be used for all tomato fields in Egypt because the decision changes with:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A correct decision is complete only when it specifies in advance:
